Order To Show Cause Re: Temporary Restraining Order; Per Judge Kahn During Ex Parte Hearing Held On 7/7/17. (302/Hk)
Off Calendar Jul-25-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Alan Stomel, Esq. (707-304-3539) for Plaintiff.
Neema Ghiasi, Esq. (415-635-0101) of Ogloza Fortney LLP specially appearing for Defendant through Court Call.

RULING- The matter is uncontested. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Plaintiff Samuel Biggs' application for a right to attach order and Order for issuance of writ of attachment is granted. No opposition filed and good cause shown. Order is signed. Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR 711, ckaren@sftc.org =(302/HK)

Alexis Wong'S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service Of Summons
Off Calendar Jul-25-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Alan Stomel, Esq. (707-304-3539) for Plaintiff.
Neema Ghiasi, Esq. (415-635-0101) of Ogloza Fortney LLP specially appearing for Defendant through Court Call.

RULING - ARGUED. The Court shares his concerns with the parties. Defendant argues Plaintiff did not attempt good faith service. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT ALEXIS WONG'S Motion To Quash Service Of Summons is DENIED. Plaintiff Samuel Biggs has shown that he was unable to ascertain Ms. Wong's Hong Kong address despite reasonable diligence, rendering the rules for service of process in the Hague Service Convention inapplicable. Mr. Biggs has also shown that his substitute service on Ms. Wong was proper. Article 1 of the Hague Service Convention states that the Convention does not apply if the address of the person to be served is not known. This provision has been construed to mean that the Hague Service Convention does not apply when a defendant's whereabouts cannot be ascertained despite reasonable diligence. (Lebel v. Mai (2012) 210 Cal.App.4th 1154, 1161; Kott v. Superior Court (1996) 45 Cal.App.4th 1126, 1139.) Mr. Biggs submitted evidence showing that Ms. Wong's address in Hong Kong was not discoverable with reasonable diligence. His attorney, Alan Stomel, conducted an internet search for Ms. Wong which showed that she was affiliated with a Hong Kong entity, but no addresses or contact information was posted for the company. Mr. Biggs thereafter consulted with a legal and investigative firm in China which conducted a preliminary search but was unable to find an address for Ms. Wong in Hong Kong. Mr. Biggs also hired a private investigation company, Keystone Investigate Services, to extensively research Ms. Wong's whereabouts. Keystone created a report of its investigation revealing that Ms. Wong currently owns residential property in San Francisco, has active telephone numbers with San Francisco area codes, and is affiliated with various California companies. Mr. Biggs justifiably believed Ms. Wong to reside in California and, given his reliance on the information from the investigative report, conducted a reasonably diligent search to locate an address for Ms. Wong in Hong Kong. Mr. Biggs has met his burden of showing that his substitute service on Ms. Wong complied with California law. Ms. Wong was substitute served at 1160 Mission Street #2213, a condominium owned by her. The concierge for the building stated that Ms. Wong was out of town and was authorized to accept packages for her. A reasonable inference from the concierge's statement is that this address is Ms. Wong's usual mailing address, which is a permissible location for substitute service and need not be either Ms. Wong's primary home or place of business. (Weil and Brown Book at 4:206-4:210). Moreover, a concierge is a permissible person that can receive substitute service. (Id. at 4:212). Ms. Wong has not submitted credible evidence contradicting any of these facts, which are sufficient to support substitute service on her. Order signed after hearing. Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR 711, ckaren@sftc.org =(302/HK)
